In social psychology, there's a classic study called the Ash Study, in which researchers had people go around a table and choose between three lines as to which was the longest. The researchers rigged the situation so that the first five people who were Confederates of the experimenter all chose the wrong line. Then when it was the sixth person's turn to choose who was a true subject, a high percentage of them went along with the crowd instead of believing their own eyes. Now, more recently, other researchers replicated that procedure with people who were hooked up to brain imaging technology. They found strong activity in two brain sectors of the true subjects. One was the area associated with puzzlement, which makes sense. But the more psychologically interesting one was the sector associated with physical pain. The experience of being out of step with one's peers didn't just produce mild psychological discomfort. It was akin to experiencing physical pain. So we can see why it's so difficult for people to be out of step with the crowd, with their group, with their subculture. It's painful to be the outlier, which helps explain why sending people social proof information that supports our position can be so effective. Now, there's one more thing about the more recent study that was instructive. Instead of arranging for the true subjects to be out of step with five other people, they had some of the true subjects be out of step with the choices of five computers. What happened to the brain activity of those true subjects? As you would expect, the brain sectors associated with puzzlement lit up, but there was no activity in the pain-related sectors. Why not? There are no social consequences of being out of step with computers, but there are some negative social consequences of being out of step with your friends or peers. Once again, we can see why social proof information is so forceful in guiding behavior.
